table_of_contents.title: From 2D image tasks to video classification and clip training
Early fusion and 3D convolution as slow fusion
Receptive-field analysis, temporal shift invariance, and 3D filter visualization
Video datasets, Sports-1M results, and C3D
Motion, optical flow, and two-stream action recognition
Long-term temporal structure with RNNs and recurrent convolution
Self-attention, I3D inflation, modern video transformers, and downstream video tasks
Multisensory, efficient, egocentric, and LLM-based video understanding

units.name: From 2D image tasks to video classification and clip training
units.description: Introduces the guest lecture, reviews 2D recognition tasks, defines video as images plus time, and explains why video models are trained on short low-resolution clips.
units.summary: The lecture starts by positioning video understanding as an extension of image recognition: images support classification, semantic segmentation, detection, and instance segmentation, but the world is not static [ts=103s] [ts=148s]. A video is defined as a sequence of frames with an extra temporal dimension, represented as T x 3 x H x W or 3 x T x H x W, so video understanding must extract features from a 4D volume rather than a single image [ts=185s] [ts=205s]. Video classification is introduced as an action-recognition problem such as distinguishing running, swimming, jumping, eating, or standing, using familiar classifiers and losses once useful video features are available [ts=217s] [ts=263s]. The segment emphasizes the data-size problem: uncompressed SD video is about 1.5 GB per minute, HD is about 10 GB per minute, and raw videos cannot be fed directly to GPUs because inputs, weights, and activations would be too large [ts=350s] [ts=374s]. The practical workaround is to shrink videos both temporally and spatially, train on short clips such as 16 frames at 112 x 112 and low FPS, sample many clips as training data, and average clip predictions at test time for long videos [ts=396s] [ts=480s] [ts=496s].

key_points.text: A video is a 2D image sequence with an added temporal dimension, commonly represented as T x 3 x H x W or 3 x T x H x W.
Video classification often recognizes actions or activities rather than static object categories.
Uncompressed video is large: roughly 1.5 GB per minute for SD and about 10 GB per minute for 1920 x 1080 HD.
A practical training input can be a short low-FPS, low-resolution clip such as T=16, H=W=112, about 3.2 seconds at 5 fps.
Long-video inference can run the model on sampled clips and average the clip-level predictions.

units.name: Single-frame CNNs and late-fusion baselines
units.description: Covers simple per-frame CNN baselines, frame sampling, and late-fusion designs that combine independently extracted frame features.
units.summary: The first baseline treats video as a collection of images: sample frames, run a normal 2D CNN on each frame, and average predicted probabilities, which can work well when frames already reveal the action [ts=509s] [ts=557s]. The instructor stresses this is often a strong baseline and should be run first before building more complex video classifiers [ts=574s] [ts=585s]. Frame sampling is itself important: random sampling may work, but selecting informative frames or adapting the next sample based on earlier samples is an active research area [ts=618s] [ts=651s]. Late fusion improves on per-frame averaging by applying a 2D CNN independently to each key frame, producing T feature maps, then either flattening and concatenating them into a large vector for an MLP or pooling over space and time before a linear classifier [ts=692s] [ts=726s] [ts=819s]. The FC version can explode in parameters because the feature vector scales with T, while pooling keeps the feature dimension D but may discard important temporal information [ts=765s] [ts=841s] [ts=862s]. The central limitation of late fusion is that low-level motion cues, such as feet moving up and down, may be lost after many convolution and pooling layers before temporal comparison happens [ts=892s] [ts=960s].

key_points.text: A single-frame CNN runs an image classifier on sampled video frames and averages predictions.
Single-frame CNNs are often a very strong baseline and should be tried first when designing a video classifier.
Frame sampling is a key design question; random sampling is simple but not necessarily the smartest strategy.
Late fusion with FC layers concatenates independently extracted frame features into a large clip feature before an MLP.
Concatenation-based late fusion can introduce many parameters when T is large.
Pooling late fusion keeps feature dimension D but may remove information important for recognition.
Late fusion can miss low-level motion because high-level feature maps may no longer retain frame-to-frame movement cues.

units.name: Early fusion and 3D convolution as slow fusion
units.description: Explains early temporal fusion by channel stacking, its limitation, and the motivation for 3D convolution and 3D pooling.
units.summary: Early fusion tries to compare frames near the input by reshaping T RGB frames into a 3T x H x W tensor and using the first 2D convolution to map the channel dimension from 3T to D [ts=979s] [ts=1002s]. After this first layer, the rest of the network is a standard 2D CNN trained with cross-entropy, but the temporal dimension has been collapsed immediately, so all temporal processing is forced into one convolutional layer [ts=1028s] [ts=1038s]. A student question clarifies that pooling in the earlier late-fusion setup averages feature vectors, producing a D-dimensional feature by mean or max pooling over features rather than pooling raw frames [ts=1046s] [ts=1061s]. The instructor then motivates 3D CNNs as a middle ground: instead of fusing at the very beginning or very end, use 3D convolution and 3D pooling to gradually combine spatial and temporal information over the course of the network [ts=1098s] [ts=1115s]. The segment reviews 2D convolution over H and W, then extends it to 3D convolution over T x H x W while still spanning the channel dimension, ending with flattened features and FC layers for class scores [ts=1132s] [ts=1201s] [ts=1273s].

key_points.text: Early fusion reshapes T frames from T x 3 x H x W into 3T x H x W.
The first 2D convolution in early fusion maps 3T channels to D and collapses temporal information immediately.
Late-fusion pooling averages or max-pools feature vectors over time, leaving a D-dimensional feature rather than pooling raw frames.
Early fusion may be too ambitious because it tries to capture all temporal information in a single layer.
A 3D CNN uses 3D convolution and 3D pooling to slowly fuse temporal information throughout the network.
3D convolution slides over a cube in T x H x W while going through the channel dimension.

units.name: Receptive-field analysis, temporal shift invariance, and 3D filter visualization
units.description: Compares late fusion, early fusion, and 3D CNNs through toy receptive-field examples and explains temporal shift invariance.
units.summary: The instructor uses toy architectures to compare how temporal receptive fields develop in late fusion, early fusion, and 3D CNNs [ts=1306s] [ts=1333s]. In late fusion, 2D layers keep the temporal dimension unchanged while building spatial receptive fields, then a global average pool collapses all time at the end [ts=1340s] [ts=1403s] [ts=1422s]. In early fusion, time is collapsed at the very beginning by treating 3 x 20 channels as one enlarged channel axis, then the network builds spatial receptive fields like a 2D CNN [ts=1440s] [ts=1466s]. In contrast, a 3D CNN builds both spatial and temporal receptive fields gradually through 3D convolutions and 3D pooling, which is why it is described as slow fusion [ts=1498s] [ts=1561s]. A deeper comparison explains that early-fusion 2D filters extend fully over time, so they lack temporal shift invariance: the same transition occurring at different time steps would need separate filters [ts=1605s] [ts=1635s] [ts=1684s]. 3D filters span only a local temporal window and slide over time, so they can reuse filters to recognize the same motion pattern at any moment, and learned 3D kernels can be visualized as video clips showing colors, edges, and motion directions [ts=1739s] [ts=1789s] [ts=1813s] [ts=1883s].

key_points.text: Late fusion builds spatial receptive field gradually but builds temporal receptive field all at once at the final global pooling layer.
Early fusion builds temporal receptive field all at once in the first layer, then builds space gradually.
3D CNNs build receptive fields gradually in both space and time, which motivates the term slow fusion.
Early-fusion 2D convolution is local in space but extends fully in time, causing no temporal shift invariance.
3D convolution uses a local temporal window that slides over time, enabling temporal shift invariance and filter reuse.
Learned 3D filters can be visualized as video clips, revealing color, edge, and motion-sensitive kernels.

units.name: Video datasets, Sports-1M results, and C3D
units.description: Discusses Sports-1M, empirical comparisons of simple and 3D models, dataset distribution issues, and the C3D architecture.
units.summary: After covering architectures, the lecture asks what data can train video classifiers, using Sports-1M as a major early dataset for fine-grained sports classification with 487 categories [ts=1921s] [ts=1939s] [ts=1972s]. Results on Sports-1M show a surprising lesson: the single-frame baseline already performs strongly, early fusion can be slightly worse, late fusion is slightly better, and a 2014-style 3D CNN gives only a modest 2–3% boost in that setting [ts=1989s] [ts=2013s] [ts=2031s]. The segment also notes video datasets are harder to distribute than ImageNet because videos are huge; Sports-1M was shared as YouTube URLs, but many videos disappear or change, making the dataset unstable over time [ts=2083s] [ts=2108s] [ts=2124s]. C3D is introduced as an early popular 3D CNN, essentially a VGG-like architecture with 3 x 3 x 3 convolutions and 2 x 2 x 2 pooling, trained on Sports-1M and widely reused as a pretrained feature extractor [ts=2149s] [ts=2179s] [ts=2227s]. Its drawback is compute: converting 2D VGG-style computation to 3D raises cost to about 39.5 GFLOPs, roughly 2.9 times VGG-16, motivating later improvements such as residual connections and better architectures [ts=2296s] [ts=2334s] [ts=2376s].

key_points.text: Sports-1M is an early large video dataset with very fine-grained sports categories, including 487 sports classes.
On Sports-1M, the single-frame baseline reaches strong top-five accuracy, early fusion is slightly worse, late fusion slightly better, and 3D CNNs add about 2–3%.
Video datasets distributed as YouTube URL lists can become unstable because videos are modified or deleted.
C3D is VGG-like but replaces 2D image operations with 3D convolution and pooling, such as 3 x 3 x 3 conv and 2 x 2 x 2 pooling.
Pretrained C3D became popular as a feature extractor for researchers who could not afford to train large video models.
C3D is computationally expensive: about 39.5 GFLOPs versus 13.6 GFLOPs for VGG-16 and 0.7 GFLOPs for AlexNet.

units.name: Motion, optical flow, and two-stream action recognition
units.description: Motivates separating appearance and motion, defines optical flow, and explains two-stream networks for action recognition.
units.summary: The lecture shifts from treating space and time uniformly to asking whether temporal information should be modeled explicitly as motion [ts=2399s] [ts=2414s]. A point-light action example demonstrates that humans can recognize actions from motion alone, without appearance information, motivating separate processing pathways for appearance and motion [ts=2420s] [ts=2463s] [ts=2477s]. Optical flow is introduced as an explicit motion representation: for each pixel in one frame, estimate how it moves to the next frame, producing a flow field F(x,y)=(dx,dy) where the next-frame location x+dx, y+dy corresponds to the current pixel [ts=2503s] [ts=2522s] [ts=2540s]. Horizontal and vertical flow components can be visualized separately and capture low-level motion cues [ts=2580s] [ts=2603s]. Two-stream networks then combine an appearance stream over RGB frames with a temporal stream over stacked optical-flow maps, aggregating their predictions [ts=2612s] [ts=2651s]. On UCF-101, motion-only performance is surprisingly strong; the instructor hypothesizes that motion is less prone to overfitting because it removes distracting background appearance and keeps key movement cues [ts=2662s] [ts=2688s] [ts=2695s].

key_points.text: Actions can often be recognized from motion alone, even with only point-like visual cues and little appearance information.
Optical flow estimates how each pixel in one frame moves to the next frame.
The flow field can be written as F(x,y) = (dx,dy), relating I_{t+1}(x+dx,y+dy) to I_t(x,y).
Horizontal flow dx and vertical flow dy can be visualized separately to show low-level motion cues.
A two-stream network uses an RGB appearance stream and a temporal stream over stacked optical-flow maps, then aggregates predictions.
On UCF-101, the temporal motion stream works surprisingly well and can outperform the spatial appearance-only stream.

units.name: Long-term temporal structure with RNNs and recurrent convolution
units.description: Explains how RNN/LSTM-style models can aggregate frame or clip features over long videos and how recurrence can be combined with convolution.
units.summary: The instructor distinguishes short-term clip modeling from long-term temporal structure, where the model must recognize relationships across distant times in long videos [ts=2722s] [ts=2739s]. A practical strategy is to extract local features with a single-frame CNN or 3D CNN and feed the feature sequence into RNNs or LSTMs, producing either a many-to-one video-level prediction at the final timestep or one prediction per frame [ts=2773s] [ts=2794s] [ts=2800s] [ts=2821s]. Training can be difficult end-to-end because the recurrent and convolutional parts form a huge network, so CNNs may be pretrained or frozen and used as feature extractors before training the recurrent module [ts=2855s] [ts=2882s]. The lecture then introduces recurrent convolutional networks: each feature map at a layer/time depends both on the same layer at the previous timestep and the previous layer at the current timestep, making a grid over time and depth [ts=2908s] [ts=2960s]. Instead of RNN matrix multiplications, recurrent convolution replaces those operations with 2D convolutions over feature maps, adds the transformed inputs, and applies tanh; the same idea can be adapted to GRUs and LSTMs [ts=3000s] [ts=3043s] [ts=3062s]. The major limitation is that RNNs are slow and hard to parallelize over long video sequences [ts=3082s] [ts=3095s].

key_points.text: Long videos require modeling temporal structure beyond the short clips handled by earlier models.
Frame or clip features from CNNs can be fed into RNNs or LSTMs to model long-term temporal structure.
RNN video models can be many-to-one for video-level classification or one-to-one for per-frame predictions.
For recurrent video models, CNNs are often pretrained or frozen as feature extractors because full end-to-end training is hard.
A recurrent convolutional feature map depends on the same layer at the previous timestep and the previous layer at the same timestep.
Recurrent convolution replaces RNN matrix multiplications with 2D convolutions over feature maps.
RNN-based video processing is slow and difficult to parallelize for long sequences.

units.name: Self-attention, I3D inflation, modern video transformers, and downstream video tasks
units.description: Covers nonlocal self-attention blocks, I3D transfer from image networks, transformer progress, video-model visualization, and localization/detection tasks.
units.summary: Because RNNs are hard to parallelize, the lecture turns to self-attention, whose query-key-value alignment can be computed in parallel and extended to video feature maps [ts=3102s] [ts=3128s] [ts=3141s]. A spatio-temporal self-attention or nonlocal block starts from C x T x H x W features, uses 1 x 1 x 1 convolutions to form query, key, and value feature maps, computes attention over query-key pairs, conditions the values, maps back to C, and adds a residual connection [ts=3149s] [ts=3170s] [ts=3201s]. Such nonlocal blocks can be inserted into 3D CNNs to fuse information across both space and time [ts=3221s] [ts=3252s]. The I3D idea asks whether successful 2D image architectures can be reused for video by replacing each 2D K_h x K_w convolution or pooling layer with a 3D K_t x K_h x K_w version, and even copying pretrained 2D weights K_t times while dividing by K_t [ts=3270s] [ts=3295s] [ts=3363s]. This inflation preserves output for a constant-frame video and gives useful image-pretrained initialization for fine-tuning video models [ts=3382s] [ts=3402s] [ts=3485s]. The segment then surveys video transformers, including factorized space-time attention, token pooling, and masked autoencoder pretraining, noting large progress on Kinetics-400 up to around 90% top-1 accuracy [ts=3500s] [ts=3526s] [ts=3550s]. Finally, it describes gradient-ascent visualization of two-stream video models and moves from clip classification to temporal action localization and spatio-temporal detection, where actions must be localized in time and possibly in both space and time [ts=3579s] [ts=3655s] [ts=3676s] [ts=3723s].

key_points.text: Self-attention is attractive for video because query-key-value alignment can be computed in parallel.
A nonlocal video block forms query, key, and value maps from C x T x H x W features using 1 x 1 x 1 3D convolutions.
Nonlocal blocks can be inserted into 3D CNNs to fuse across both space and time.
I3D inflates image CNNs by replacing each 2D K_h x K_w operation with a 3D K_t x K_h x K_w operation.
Inflated 3D kernels can be initialized by copying a 2D kernel K_t times and dividing by K_t.
Modern video transformers use ideas such as factorized space-time attention, token pooling, and masked autoencoder pretraining.
Video models can be visualized by gradient ascent on appearance and flow inputs to maximize a class score.
Temporal action localization identifies when actions occur; spatio-temporal detection localizes activities in both space and time.

units.name: Multisensory, efficient, egocentric, and LLM-based video understanding
units.description: Concludes with audio-visual video understanding, source separation, efficient clip selection, egocentric multimodal streams, and video foundation models.
units.summary: The final segment revisits the opening baby/dog video and points out a missing modality: video often includes sound, which carries emotions, interactions, and additional cues beyond visual motion [ts=3759s] [ts=3790s] [ts=3793s]. Audio-visual tasks include visually guided audio source separation, where visual information guides decomposition of a mixed sound into components such as individual speakers or instruments [ts=3828s] [ts=3848s] [ts=3861s] [ts=3878s]. Audio can also help video classification: transformer-style models can map both image/video patches and audio spectrogram patches into multimodal architectures, including masked-autoencoder objectives over images and spectrograms [ts=3891s] [ts=3904s] [ts=3921s]. The lecture then shifts to efficient video understanding: because long videos cannot be processed clip by clip exhaustively, methods try to build efficient single-clip models, sample salient clips, or learn policies deciding which clips and modalities such as video or audio to use [ts=3928s] [ts=3954s] [ts=3974s] [ts=3995s]. Audio can act as a preview mechanism to identify important moments before visual processing [ts=4006s] [ts=4020s]. The concluding research directions include multimodal egocentric video from smart glasses with camera and microphone arrays, predicting social interactions such as who is speaking to whom, and connecting video understanding to LLM-style foundation models that tokenize video and generate text descriptions [ts=4030s] [ts=4065s] [ts=4080s] [ts=4107s].

key_points.text: Audio is an important modality in videos, adding sound, emotion, and interaction cues missing from silent visual streams.
Visually guided audio source separation uses visual information to separate a sound mixture into components.
Speech separation can use audio-visual information to isolate the voice of each visible speaker.
Instrument separation can use motion and object-centric visual information together with audio.
Audio-visual transformer models can process both video patches and audio spectrogram patches.
Efficient video understanding is needed because long videos cannot be processed by running every clip one by one.
Efficient methods may select salient clips or learn policies for which modality and how many clips to process.
Egocentric multimodal video and video-to-LLM foundation models are active research directions.

concepts_kp_local.name: Video as a 4D tensor
concepts_kp_local.description: A video is a sequence of image frames with an explicit temporal dimension, represented as T x 3 x H x W or 3 x T x H x W.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: This representation underlies every later architecture and task in the lecture.
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: Slide/visual transition around [ts=185s] shows video as 2D plus time.

concepts_kp_local.name: Clip-based training and test-time averaging
concepts_kp_local.description: Long videos are handled by training on short low-FPS, low-resolution clips and averaging sampled clip predictions at inference.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: It is the practical entry point for making video classification computationally feasible.
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: Slides around [ts=456s] illustrate raw video, training clips, and averaged test clips.

concepts_kp_local.name: Single-frame CNN baseline
concepts_kp_local.description: A simple video classifier can sample frames, run a 2D CNN independently, and average probabilities.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: The instructor explicitly warns to try this strong baseline before complex models.
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: The slide around [ts=574s] shows repeated CNNs producing running predictions.

concepts_kp_local.name: Late fusion
concepts_kp_local.description: Late fusion extracts high-level per-frame features independently and combines them near the classifier via concatenation or pooling.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: It is the first architectural step beyond frame averaging and exposes trade-offs in temporal fusion.
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: Slides around [ts=692s] show frame CNNs, flatten/pooling, and class scores.

concepts_kp_local.name: Early fusion
concepts_kp_local.description: Early fusion stacks frames along channels so the first 2D convolution compares frames and collapses time.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: It clarifies why fusing all temporal information at one input layer can be limiting.
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: The slide around [ts=988s] shows reshape to 3T x H x W and the first 2D convolution.

concepts_kp_local.name: 3D CNN and slow fusion
concepts_kp_local.description: 3D CNNs use 3D convolution and pooling to gradually fuse spatial and temporal information across layers.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: This is one of the lecture’s central mechanisms for learning motion-sensitive video features.
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: The slide around [ts=1103s] shows a 3D CNN over 3 x T x H x W inputs.

concepts_kp_local.name: Temporal receptive field and shift invariance
concepts_kp_local.description: 3D convolution uses local temporal filters that slide over time, enabling temporal shift invariance unlike early fusion.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: It explains the architectural reason 3D convolution is more parameter-efficient for motion patterns than fully temporal 2D filters.
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: Toy receptive-field slides around [ts=1340s]-[ts=1789s] compare late, early, and 3D fusion.

concepts_kp_local.name: Sports-1M and video dataset instability
concepts_kp_local.description: Sports-1M is a fine-grained sports video dataset originally distributed as YouTube URLs, making it vulnerable to deleted or modified videos.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: The dataset grounds empirical comparisons and exposes practical problems in video dataset distribution.
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: Examples around [ts=1939s] show fine-grained sports predictions.

concepts_kp_local.name: C3D
concepts_kp_local.description: C3D is an early VGG-like 3D CNN trained on Sports-1M and widely used as a pretrained feature extractor, but it is compute-heavy.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: It illustrates both the promise and compute cost of straightforward 3D convolutional video models.
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: Slides around [ts=2149s] compare C3D architecture and GFLOPs.

concepts_kp_local.name: Optical flow
concepts_kp_local.description: Optical flow estimates per-pixel motion between adjacent frames as horizontal and vertical displacement fields.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: It provides the explicit motion representation needed for the two-stream architecture.
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: Slides around [ts=2503s] visualize optical-flow fields and dx/dy components.

concepts_kp_local.name: Two-stream networks
concepts_kp_local.description: Two-stream action networks combine an RGB appearance stream with an optical-flow motion stream and aggregate predictions.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: It formalizes separating appearance and motion, a major action-recognition idea in the lecture.
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: The slide around [ts=2612s] shows separate appearance and temporal streams.

concepts_kp_local.name: Long-term temporal modeling with RNNs/LSTMs
concepts_kp_local.description: Frame or clip features can be fed to recurrent models to capture long-range dependencies and produce video-level or frame-level outputs.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: This extends clip recognition to long videos and connects video to sequence modeling.
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: Slides around [ts=2773s] show CNN feature sequences flowing into recurrent models.

concepts_kp_local.name: Recurrent convolutional networks
concepts_kp_local.description: Recurrent convolutional networks combine recurrence with spatial feature maps by replacing recurrent matrix operations with convolutions.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: It shows one way to fuse spatial and temporal processing, even though parallelization limits use.
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: Slides around [ts=2924s] show feature-map dependencies over time and layers.

concepts_kp_local.name: Spatio-temporal self-attention and nonlocal blocks
concepts_kp_local.description: Nonlocal blocks extend self-attention to C x T x H x W video feature maps using query, key, and value projections.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: It connects prior self-attention concepts to video and gives a parallel alternative to RNNs.
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: Slides around [ts=3149s] diagram query/key/value maps and a residual nonlocal block.

concepts_kp_local.name: I3D inflation and weight transfer
concepts_kp_local.description: I3D converts 2D image networks to video by inflating kernels and pooling layers to 3D and reusing pretrained image weights.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: It is a practical bridge from strong image architectures to video architectures.
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: Slides around [ts=3295s] show 2D-to-3D kernel inflation and copying weights.

concepts_kp_local.name: Video transformers and masked autoencoders
concepts_kp_local.description: Modern video models use factorized attention, token pooling, and masked-autoencoder pretraining for scalable video understanding.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: It surveys the modern successor family to CNN-based video models.
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: Slides around [ts=3500s] list video transformer and VideoMAE-style methods.

concepts_kp_local.name: Temporal localization and spatio-temporal detection
concepts_kp_local.description: Beyond clip classification, video understanding can localize actions in time and detect people/actions jointly in space and time.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: It broadens the lecture from classification architectures to downstream structured prediction tasks.
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: Slides around [ts=3676s] show temporal proposals and spatio-temporal detection examples.

concepts_kp_local.name: Audio-visual source separation
concepts_kp_local.description: Visual cues can guide separation of mixed audio into sources such as speakers or instruments.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: It demonstrates that video understanding can require multisensory fusion beyond the visual stream.
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: Video/audio examples around [ts=3828s] demonstrate speech and instrument separation.

concepts_kp_local.name: Efficient video understanding
concepts_kp_local.description: Efficient methods reduce computation through better clip models, salient clip sampling, and adaptive modality selection.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: The lecture emphasizes efficiency because long videos cannot be exhaustively processed clip by clip.
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: Slides around [ts=3928s] show efficient recognition methods including X3D, SCSampler, and adaptive multimodal learning.

concepts_kp_local.name: Egocentric multimodal video and video LLMs
concepts_kp_local.description: Emerging video understanding includes smart-glasses egocentric streams, multichannel audio, and models that connect video to LLMs.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: It closes with active research directions rather than core techniques used throughout the lecture.
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: Final slides around [ts=4030s]-[ts=4115s] show egocentric sensing and video-LLM examples.

unit_kp_map_local.coverage_rationale: The unit defines video as 2D plus time and gives tensor forms [ts=185s].
The unit explains clip training and prediction averaging for long videos [ts=456s] [ts=496s].
The unit explains single-frame CNNs and says to run them first as a baseline [ts=574s] [ts=585s].
The unit details late fusion by concatenation or pooling and its drawbacks [ts=692s] [ts=862s].
The unit defines early fusion by reshaping to 3T x H x W and collapsing time in the first conv layer [ts=988s] [ts=1038s].
The unit introduces 3D CNNs as slow fusion with 3D convolution and pooling [ts=1098s] [ts=1132s].
Early fusion is revisited in the receptive-field comparison [ts=1427s] [ts=1493s].
3D CNNs are compared against early and late fusion as slow fusion [ts=1498s] [ts=1566s].
The unit explains temporal shift invariance through local temporal filters sliding over time [ts=1635s] [ts=1769s].
The unit introduces Sports-1M as a fine-grained dataset and discusses URL instability [ts=1939s] [ts=2108s].
The unit describes C3D architecture, pretrained use, and compute cost [ts=2149s] [ts=2296s].
Sports-1M results reinforce that single-frame baselines can be strong [ts=1994s] [ts=2013s].
The unit defines optical flow and its pixel-displacement formula [ts=2503s] [ts=2540s].
The unit builds two-stream networks from separate appearance and flow streams [ts=2612s] [ts=2658s].
The unit explains feeding CNN frame or clip features to RNN/LSTM models [ts=2773s] [ts=2794s].
The unit defines recurrent convolutional networks and their feature-map recurrence [ts=2908s] [ts=3050s].
The unit extends self-attention to video feature maps with nonlocal blocks [ts=3149s] [ts=3235s].
The unit describes I3D layer inflation and pretrained weight copying [ts=3270s] [ts=3402s].
The unit surveys video transformers, factorized attention, and masked autoencoders [ts=3500s] [ts=3534s].
The unit covers visualizing video models and tasks beyond clip classification, including localization and detection [ts=3579s] [ts=3723s].
The unit explains visually guided audio source separation for speech and instruments [ts=3828s] [ts=3886s].
The unit motivates efficient video understanding and salient clip or modality selection [ts=3928s] [ts=3995s].
The unit closes with egocentric multimodal video and video-LLM research directions [ts=4030s] [ts=4115s].

section_flags.rationale: Foundational framing: defines video tensors, action classification, video size constraints, and clip-based training [ts=185s] [ts=480s].
Covers practical baselines and late-fusion trade-offs that are easy to test in questions [ts=574s] [ts=862s].
Introduces early fusion formulas and 3D convolution, including tensor reshaping and C x T x H x W operations [ts=988s] [ts=1206s].
Receptive-field and temporal shift-invariance reasoning is conceptually central and formula/architecture-heavy [ts=1340s] [ts=1769s].
Empirical dataset/model comparison and C3D compute details are important but less foundational than fusion mechanisms [ts=1939s] [ts=2296s].
Optical flow formula and two-stream architecture are central motion-modeling concepts [ts=2503s] [ts=2658s].
Long-term RNN and recurrent-convolution ideas are important for sequencing but presented as limited by parallelization [ts=2773s] [ts=3082s].
Dense architecture survey: nonlocal attention, I3D, transformers, visualization, and localization/detection [ts=3149s] [ts=3723s].
Concluding research directions include audio-visual examples, efficiency, egocentric streams, and video LLMs [ts=3828s] [ts=4115s].
